title: Butternut squash soup
subtitle: Roasted squash blended with chilli and coconut milk.
excerpt: Half a butternut squash roasted with onion until caramelised, then blended with vegetable stock and coconut milk into a silky, gently spiced soup. The coconut milk softens the spice and gives the soup body without heaviness.
sourceNotes: Butternut squash arrived in British supermarkets in significant volumes through the 1980s, and butternut squash soup became a fixture of British home cooking and café menus through the 1990s. The coconut milk variant, drawing on Thai and South-East Asian flavour combinations, entered the mainstream home-cook repertoire by the early 2000s.

content.text: Butternut squash soup improves dramatically when the squash goes into a hot oven before it goes into a pot. Roasting drives off moisture, concentrates the natural sugars, and adds colour that no amount of simmering achieves. Cut the squash into chunks, roast for 35 minutes, and the hardest work is done.

The coconut milk is optional but recommended. It rounds off the spice, adds creaminess, and balances the squash's sweetness. A tin of light coconut milk is enough; full-fat makes the soup richer but heavier.

items.name: Butternut squash
items.prepNote: about 900 g, peeled, deseeded, cut into 3 cm chunks

items.name: Red chilli
items.prepNote: deseeded for mild, kept whole for more heat

content.text: A large roasting tin, a large saucepan, and a stick blender are what you need. If your tin is too crowded, the squash will steam rather than roast.

Heat the oven to 200 °C fan. Spread the squash chunks, onion, {{garlic}} of unpeeled garlic cloves, ginger slices, and chilli (if using) across a large roasting tin. Drizzle with {{olive-oil}} of olive oil and toss well. Season with {{sea-salt-flakes}} of sea salt. Roast for 35 to 40 minutes until the squash is tender and caramelised at the edges.
Squeeze the garlic from their skins. Transfer the roasted vegetables to a large saucepan. Pour over {{stock-vegetable}} of hot vegetable stock. Blend with a stick blender until completely smooth.
Place the pan over a medium heat and stir in {{coconut-milk}} of coconut milk. Warm through gently without boiling. Taste and adjust seasoning. Serve with fresh coriander.

items.fix: Add a squeeze of lime juice and a pinch more salt. A very small amount of chilli helps too.
items.cause: Butternut squash can be very high in natural sugars, especially in autumn.

items.fix: Add the coconut milk off direct high heat, or reduce the heat to a gentle simmer before adding it. A split does not affect flavour; just stir vigorously.
items.cause: The coconut milk was added to a boiling soup and then boiled hard.
items.symptom: Soup splits after adding coconut milk

items.fix: Spread vegetables in a single layer next time. For this batch, a few extra minutes under the grill will add colour.
items.cause: Roasting tin was too crowded, causing steaming rather than roasting.
items.symptom: Squash is soft but pale, not caramelised

items.fix: Pass through a sieve. For future batches, slice ginger thinly and roast for the full time.
items.cause: Squash was very fibrous, or ginger not fully blended.

content.text: Butternut squash soup is one of the soups most tied to British autumn home cooking. The squash is a North American vegetable. It reached British supermarkets in real numbers through the 1980s. It found a quick home in the season's soup line-up because the flesh is sweet and easy to blend, better than most other squash types. The coconut milk version is now the leading British home-cook form. The plain-stock version has given way to one with more depth.
